frontmatter.title: Mysteries of Deep Learning
frontmatter.summary: A survey of the empirical mysteries of deep learning — generalization despite overparameterization, optimization on non-convex landscapes, representational alignment, and in-context learning — and candidate explanations including the program-synthesis hypothesis.
learningOutcomes: Students can explain how the three barriers from Day B.1 (approximation, generalization, optimization) manifest specifically in the context of deep learning, and why classical theoretical frameworks (e.g. bias-variance tradeoff, universal approximation) actively counterpredicted deep learning's success

Students are aware of the key empirical mysteries of deep learning: data-dependent generalization despite overparameterization, effectiveness of SGD on non-convex landscapes, representational alignment across architectures, and in-context learning
Students have encountered at least one candidate explanation for each mystery and can articulate what it does and doesn't explain
Students understand the "program synthesis" hypothesis as one proposed framework connecting deep learning to Solomonoff induction, and can evaluate its strengths and limitations

Students can articulate why solving these mysteries matters for AI safety: understanding the basic mechanisms by which deep learning works is necessary for any *systematic* (generalizing OOD) alignment interventions or measurements to even be possible

frontmatter.summary: A curated reading list of background worldview material and technical prerequisites (math, CS, deep learning) recommended before the Iliad Intensive.

An opinionated tour of the AI alignment problem: alignment targets, problem decompositions (outer/inner alignment, training stories, inductive biases), goal-directedness and instrumental convergence, the risk landscape, and high-level solution approaches.
frontmatter.teachingGuide:
The first 2.5 hours of this day are devoted to welcoming the participants and having introductory 1-1's. This leaves 4.0 hours to the actual content, split into four sessions.

First, explain to the students that this is mostly a conceptual day: The aim is to understand a basic decomposition of the problem of safe AI into specifying an alignment target and solving the technical alignment problem, which can itself decompose further. We also discuss the basic role that goal-directedness plays in the risks, and basic views on the risk landscape. The aim is that after this day, all students are broadly on the same page about what the alignment problem is, and have some basic impression of how the rest of the course relates to the problem.

Students who are bored since they know the content already can read all texts in "main content" that they do not know.

### Session 1: Alignment targets

Spend 40 minutes reading the following texts:

- [Coherent extrapolated volition](https://intelligence.org/files/CEV.pdf): Read the 2.5 intro pages in Section 3.
- [Intent alignment](https://ai-alignment.com/clarifying-ai-alignment-cec47cd69dd6): Read entirely.
- [Claude's constitution](https://www.anthropic.com/news/claude-new-constitution): Read the entire overview blogpost.
- [Corrigibility](https://www.lesswrong.com/w/corrigibility-1): Read entirely.

Discuss in groups of 3-5 for 20 minutes. Possible prompts:

- Which alignment targets imply which others? Where this is not the case, think through counter examples.
- Which alignment targets seem sufficient for safety? E.g., a corrigible system might cause harm before we change its values. Can it cause catastrophic harm? What about the other notions?
- What other ideas for alignment targets do you have? Does your target have a benefit?

### Session 2: Alignment problem decompositions

Spend 40 minutes reading the following texts:

- [Reward misspecification](https://deepmind.google/blog/specification-gaming-the-flip-side-of-ai-ingenuity/): Read ~entirely.
- [Goal misgeneralization](https://deepmind.google/blog/how-undesired-goals-can-arise-with-correct-rewards/): Read ~entirely.
- [Training stories](https://www.alignmentforum.org/posts/FDJnZt8Ks2djouQTZ/how-do-we-become-confident-in-the-safety-of-a-machine): Read everything before the section "How mechanistic does [...]"
- [Inductive biases](https://www.cs.cmu.edu/~tom/pubs/NeedForBias_1980.pdf): Read ~entirely

Discuss in groups of 3-5 for 20 minutes. Possible prompts:

- Think of well-known or imagined misalignment scenarios: Can you think of some that clearly fall into the reward misspecification or goal misgeneralization category? Are some not clearly in either of them?
- What problem with "outer" and "inner" misalignment is the terminology of "training stories" trying to solve? Construct examples where training stories seem more appropriate than inner/outer alignment as a framework.
- Think of concrete ways in which modern deep learning systems generalize beyond their explicit training examples. What inductive biases may underlie these examples? Is it always possible to come up with a "clean description" of those biases? Is the inductive bias you come up with enforced in the architecture, or more implicit?
    - Perhaps: Discuss this explicitly for the case of "emergent misalignment".

### Session 3: Goal directedness

Spend 40 minutes reading the following texts:

- [Why tool AIs want to be agent AIs](https://gwern.net/tool-ai): Spend as much time as you can with the post, perhaps using an LLM of your choice to summarize key points.
- [Basic AI drives](https://selfawaresystems.com/wp-content/uploads/2008/01/ai_drives_final.pdf): Read at least abstract, intro, conclusion, and understand every heading and a basic argument for its underlying AI drive.

Discuss in groups of 3-5 for 20 minutes. Possible prompts:

- Clearly we can imagine *different types* of agents, e.g. some that coherently optimize a utility function, and others that are based on contextually activated computations, etc. What type do we see in today's LLM agents? How will this development continue in the future?
- Can you think of other convergent instrumental goals than the ones mentioned in basic AI drives?
- What is the relationship between corrigibility and convergent instrumental subgoals?

### Session 4: Uncertain risks and outlook

Spend 40 minutes reading the following texts:

- [Core views on AI safety](https://www.anthropic.com/news/core-views-on-ai-safety): Read the three sections "Our Approach: Empiricism in AI Safety", "The role of frontier models in AI safety" and "Taking a portfolio approach to AI safety".
- [Model organisms of misalignment](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/ChDH335ckdvpxXaXX/model-organisms-of-misalignment-the-case-for-a-new-pillar-of-1): Read everything before "Examples of Model Organisms [...]".
- [The rocket alignment problem](https://www.alignmentforum.org/posts/Gg9a4y8reWKtLe3Tn/the-rocket-alignment-problem): Skim if time permits.

Discuss in groups of 3-5 for 20 minutes. Possible prompts:

- How large is the risk? Do we live in an optimistic, intermediate, or pessimistic scenario? How has the evidence changed in the past years?
- Can you think of model organisms for misalignment that were published after the post came out in 2023? Does this change our understanding of the risks?
- Does the analogy between the AI alignment and rocket alignment problems feel accurate? In what ways does it or does it not?
- What safety approaches (e.g., on a spectrum from theoretical to empirical) feel most appealing to you, and why? Does this depend on details of the problem you are trying to solve?
learningOutcomes: understand the basic decomposition of risks into AI misalignment, misuse, power grabs, and others
can explain different alignment targets like coherent extrapolated volition, intent alignment, or AI that follows a constitution
can reason about training stories and outer and inner (mis)alignment, challenges to these notions, and the relationship to inductive biases
are aware of discussions on whether AI systems develop goals and understand the basic arguments for instrumental convergence
are aware of foundational discussions on the level of risk and different high-level approaches to solving the AI alignment problem

headings.text: Theoretical vs. empirical vs. other approaches

frontmatter.summary: A tour of how frontier LLMs are actually aligned in practice — interventions at pre-training, post-training, and three stages of deployment — including methods, limitations, and the structure of safety cases.
frontmatter.teachingGuide:
The day is structured around five modules that walk students through the stages at which a frontier model is built and released — pretraining, post-training, and three stages of deployment — and at each stage, surface the safety-relevant ideas, methods, and open problems.

Morning: 15min intro, 1h pre-training, 1h 15min post-training

Afternoon: 30m deployment 1, 1h design discussion, 30m break, 1h deployment 2, 1h deployment 3, 15m closing session

Each module follows the same shape: a short discussion, then a presentation. The presentation walks through the main methods and ideas in narrative order, but it lands much harder if students have already encountered the underlying problems on their own. That is what the discussion is for. The methods used in practice are often simple enough that students can reinvent them, at least structurally, by being walked through the right sequence of questions. So in each discussion the instructor sets up a scenario, asks a leading question, and steers the conversation toward the methods the lecture will then formalize. When a student lands on a real method, the instructor names it, writes it on a sticky note, and puts it on the board, so the lecture can later point back at the student-generated material and connect it explicitly to the formal version. Concrete prompts and steering moves are written into each module's discussion section.

One practical issue: in any group there will be students who have already read some of the relevant papers and know the answers to the leading questions. If they speak first, the rest of the room never gets to do the work the discussion is designed to make them do. The recommended fix is to run each discussion question in small groups. When the instructor poses a question or opens a new block, the groups discuss it among themselves for a few minutes; the instructor then picks one group at random and asks them to nominate a representative to share the group's idea. Students who already know an answer should be told upfront that their job inside the group is to engage their peers and help them rediscover it, not to spoil it. This keeps the discussion productive for everyone and prevents the strongest students from accidentally collapsing it.

### Pre-training

The session is 20 minutes of discussion followed by 40 minutes of presentation.

**Opening.** Briefly outline the story of pre-training: the model is about to see the entire internet. It's forming a view of what the world is — including what AI is and how AI assistants behave. We can intervene in what it learns, which influences both its factual knowledge and its character. But we're operating before seeing the model's behavior, so every intervention is a bet.

**Discussion (30 min).** The goal is to get students to independently discover the main categories of pre-training safety interventions before the lecture formalizes them. When a student proposes something that matches a real method, name it, write it on a sticky note, and put it on the board. Do the same with limitations they identify.

Open by setting the scenario: "You want to train a model that is helpful, but which users cannot use to obtain dangerous information about bioweapon construction. You can change anything about the pre-training process — add data, remove data, change the specifics of training. What do you do? What difficulties will arise? We want around five rough ideas, not one polished one." If nobody starts after a minute, hint: "Start with: what could you remove from the dataset?"

*Block 1: Removing dangerous knowledge.* Let students propose data filtering. Validate it, then probe: "How do you know what to filter? What if your classifier misses something? What if dangerous information is embedded in an otherwise valuable document?" When someone proposes something architectural — routing knowledge to a removable part of the network, or masking gradients — name it as gradient routing / SGTM. If nobody gets there, ask: "Instead of trying to perfectly classify every document, could you change how the model *stores* what it learns?". In a similar way, see if anybody comes up with token-level filtering.

*Block 2: Shaping character.* Steer the conversation away from facts toward disposition: "Suppose you've done a good job filtering bioweapons data. But the model has also read thousands of sci-fi stories about evil AIs, forum posts about AI misalignment, and jailbreak tutorials. How might that affect its behavior — even on topics that have nothing to do with bioweapons?" Let students discover that what the model reads about AI shapes its own behavioral tendencies. Then ask: "Can you use this deliberately — can you shape the model's character by choosing what AI-related content it trains on?" Steer toward alignment pretraining. If students ask *why* this works, introduce the persona selection model: the model is learning a repertoire of personas from its data, and you're shaping the prior over which persona it adopts.

**Presentation (30 min).** Walk through the content section, following the slides. Repeat the overarching story: pre-training is where the model's deep representations are built — both its knowledge and its proto-character. Whenever you reach a method that students proposed during the discussion, point to their sticky note on the board and make the connection explicit. Present the methods in narrative order: data filtering addresses the obvious problem; gradient routing / SGTM addresses filtering's failure mode; alignment pretraining shifts from knowledge to character; PSM explains why character-shaping works. Close by repeating the overarching story one more time, and naming what pre-training can't do: you're operating before seeing the model's behavior, the feedback loop is slow, and you can't anticipate every failure mode. This motivates the next slot.

### Post-training

The session is 30 minutes of discussion followed by 45 minutes of presentation.

**Opening.** Briefly outline the story of post-training: even a small amount of compute produces shallow changes (parameter-wise, mostly chaining existing heuristics), but these can be behaviorally large — the model's outputs can look markedly different.

**Discussion (30 min).** The goal is to get students to reinvent the actual methods used in practice. When a student proposes something that matches a real method, name it ("this is what OpenAI calls Confessions"), write it on a sticky note, and put it on the board. Guide the conversation through three blocks:

*Block 1: Deliberative alignment and confessions.* Set up the scenario: you've already done some post-training and have a reasoning model that generates a long chain of thought before answering. You notice it doesn't always follow your company's safety policies. If you put all the policies into its context window, it tends to follow them, though it sometimes wastes time on irrelevant ones. Ask: what can you do? Steer toward deliberative alignment (train the model to consult the relevant policies in its CoT) and confessions (train the model to self-report whether it followed the policies, with a separate honesty reward).

*Block 2: Constitutional AI and emergent misalignment.* Remind students that the model learned many personas during pre-training, and post-training influences which one it adopts. Ask: what happens if you fine-tune on data where the model writes insecure code without telling the user? Let them discover emergent misalignment — the model adopts a broadly misaligned persona. Then ask: what if the user explicitly asks for insecure code? Let them discover inoculation prompting — the misalignment disappears. Explain that one reason why inoculation prompting works could be that we shift from training the model to have the "gets asked for safe code, provides unsafe code => evil" persona to rather have a "gets asked for unsafe code => writes unsafe code => helpful" persona. Then ask: can we use this persona-shaping deliberately, for alignment? Steer toward Constitutional AI.

*Block 3: Problems with unlearning.* Set up the scenario: your model knows how to synthesize bioweapons, and you fine-tune it to remove that knowledge by maximizing its loss on bioweapon-related data. Ask: how do you measure whether the removal worked? Steer toward the answer that fine-tuning the knowledge back in is the right robustness metric (as a proxy for what a red-teamer would do). Then ask: how robust do you expect this to be? Steer toward the answer: not very, because unlearning with little compute only makes surface-level changes on top of the vast representations built during pre-training.

**Presentation (45 min).** Walk through the content section, supported by the slides. Repeat the overarching story: post-training is "leveraged" training on top of what already exists in the model — chaining heuristics, shifting representations. Whenever you reach a method that students proposed during the discussion, point to their sticky note on the board and make the connection explicit. Try to link the methods into a coherent narrative (e.g., confessions builds on the same CoT infrastructure as deliberative alignment; inoculation prompting explains why the educational control in the EM paper didn't produce misalignment). Close by repeating the high-level story one more time.

### Deployment 1: Strategy

The session is 20 minutes of discussion followed by 20 minutes of presentation. Unlike the other deployment modules, this one is not trying to get students to reinvent technical methods — the content is a taxonomy of what deployment-time alignment actually involves, and the discussion is there to make the scope of that taxonomy feel real before the lecture formalizes it.

**Opening.** Briefly outline the shift: the model is trained, post-training is done, and the question is no longer how to shape the model but whether and how to put it into the world. Tell the students that this module is the framing module for the deployment section and that the next two go deep on specific pieces of it. Do not spoil the four-step structure yet.

**Discussion (20 min).** The goal is to get students to feel that deployment-time alignment is much larger than they initially think. Open with a single broad question: "You've finished training the model. Everything we've covered so far — pretraining, post-training — is done. Your RL pipeline has shut down. What is there left to do before this model can be released into the world?" Let students throw out answers. The first few will be obvious (run benchmarks, red-team it, write a system card).

When the room stalls, use the regulator prompt as a generator: "Now imagine an external regulator, or another lab's safety team, is going to read whatever you produce. What would they want to see that you haven't mentioned yet?" This should unlock a second wave — documentation, audit trails, external evaluations, ongoing reporting, compliance with legal frameworks, incident response procedures, the deployment context being spelled out explicitly. Keep writing contributions on the board as they come.

Once the room has clearly slowed down, make the pivot that closes the discussion: point out that almost nothing students listed is about the model's weights. It is all about the system around the model and the argument that surrounds that system. Then pose the closing question without waiting for a full answer: "How would you actually hold all of this together? If you had to hand one document to that regulator, what shape would it be?" Let one or two students sketch ideas — they may propose something organized by risk, by tier, by timeline. Do not resolve the question. Tell them the lecture is about the shape real labs have converged on, and move into the presentation.

**Presentation (20 min).** Walk through the content section. This module is more lecture-heavy than the other two, and that is deliberate — the content is a frame rather than a set of methods, and the frame needs to be stated cleanly to be useful. Open by answering the unresolved question from the discussion: the shape real labs have converged on is something close to a *safety case*.

Then walk through the four steps in order: identifying risks, understanding the model's contribution, building and stress-testing the safety system, communicating and maintaining the case. For each step, point back at the board and show which of the students' contributions live inside that step.

Close by restating the thesis in fresh language: deployment is the work of building and maintaining a structured argument that release is acceptable, the safety case is the artifact that argument lives in, the four steps are how it gets built, and none of it is ever finished because the world the model goes into keeps changing. Hand off to Deployment 2 (what the model can do and how it is used) and Deployment 3 (the safety system and how it holds up under pressure).

### Design Challenge: Red-Blue debate

The session runs after Deployment 1, before Deployment 2 and 3. It is 20 minutes of preparation, an hour of structured debate, and about 15 minutes of evaluation and closing.

**The setup.** Students are split into three teams. Blue Team (5 people) plays the role of a lab preparing to deploy a new frontier model; Red Team (5 people) plays external red-teamers trying to break the deployment; Judges (remaining people) evaluate the debate at the end.

The TA gives everyone a fixed deployment scenario — a reasoning model will be deployed as a general-purpose assistant with chat, API, and FT API access. The goal is to design the system in a way that it doesn't provide meaningful uplift on developing bioweapons.

Blue's deliverable is a technical design for the full pipeline: one primary method from the lectures per stage (pre-training, post-training, deployment), justified by the specific threats each component is meant to address. The design is not itself a safety case — Blue is not running evaluations or producing evidence — but it is the kind of system on top of which a safety case could later be built, and Blue should be able to say what evidence they would eventually need to collect to argue the deployment is safe.

Red's deliverable is a concrete attack scenario that reaches the harmful outcome — a walk-through of an attacker using Blue's deployment surface to extract the dangerous capability, with explicit reasoning about why each of Blue's three defenses fails to stop the attack. Alternatively, the Red can try arguing that the context the Blue is defending against is not broad enough and misses real risks. Finally, Red can concede that the system is safe, but in such a way that makes it completely impractical for the everyday user (e.g. imagine if Blue has a team of bio experts manually reading all conversations before passing them on).

The debate runs over two rounds of exchange, with Red going second each time so they can attack what Blue actually proposed.

**Point of the exercise.** The methods covered in the morning and early afternoon are deceptively simple. In the lecture they all seem natural and obvious, which is exactly the problem — students will remember the shape of each method but not the details, and will leave without having done the cognitive work of reasoning about a system. The debate forces that work. Blue has to commit to specific methods and defend the choices; Red has to commit to a concrete failure mechanism rather than vague "what if?" objections; both sides have to engage with details they would otherwise skim. The goal is not to invent new methods. It is to take the methods from the lectures and push on them hard enough that the subtleties surface.

A secondary goal is giving students practice reasoning at the system level rather than the method level. Blue is designing something whose components have to fit together and whose safety properties have to be articulable; Red is doing what internal and external red teams do in practice. Sitting the exercise after D1 is deliberate: students have just been given the vocabulary for thinking about deployment systems (claim, evidence, structured argument, effort-bounded safety), and the debate lets them use it on something concrete before D2 and D3 fill in the tools they will wish they had.

**Structure.** The debate is strictly timed, with slides driving the timer (one phase per slide). The phases are: 20 min prep, A1 (8) + cross-Qs (3), think break (5), B1 (8) + cross-Qs (3), think break (5), A2 (5) + cross-Qs (3), B2 (5), judges' deliberation (5), judge summaries and TA closing (~15). A1 and A2 are Blue's two speakers; B1 and B2 are Red's. A1 presents Blue's initial design; B1 presents Red's attack scenario(s) against it; A2 responds with concessions and modifications; B2 delivers a final attack showing the system still doesn't hold. Cross-Qs are for clarification, not argument — speakers ask to fill in missing details they need, and the opposing speaker answers without defending. Only designated speakers talk during their slot.

At the end, each judge speaks for about two minutes, naming the strongest attack they heard, the strongest defense, and what both teams missed. The TA closes last.

**Rules that do the work.** Three rules carry the exercise and should be on the slides, not just in the TA's head.

1. One primary method per stage for Blue — this blocks the "throw everything at the wall" failure mode and forces real commitments that Red can attack.
2. Attacks must be concrete and composed for Red — a single end-to-end scenario that walks through all three of Blue's defenses, not three disconnected what-ifs.
3. Prefer the weakest adversary that breaks the system for Red — a chat-only user finding a gap is a stronger argument than a state actor with stolen weights.

**Moves during the debate.** The TA nor the judges should intervene during speeches or cross-Qs.

**Judging and debrief.** Give judges a 5-minute deliberation window before they speak; without it, their summaries will repeat whichever argument was freshest. Each judge gets about two minutes. They speak before the TA, and the TA's closing is structural rather than evaluative — do not declare a winner. The debrief's job is to name what neither team caught, explicitly connect the missing pieces to what D2 and D3 will cover. If the debate revealed a specific misunderstanding of a lecture method, correct it briefly.

**Why this placement.** Running this after D1 rather than at the end of the day is intentional. Students hit walls during the debate — they will not know whether their auditing is good enough, whether their runtime classifier will hold, whether their adversary model is realistic — and those walls become the hooks that D2 and D3 hang on. The afternoon lectures then land as answers to questions the students have already asked themselves. Closing the debate with "here is what you still do not know; we will spend the afternoon filling those gaps" makes the exercise a motivated preview rather than a capstone, and that framing is what gives the rest of the day its weight. Running this before the break also allows the students to continue with the debate during the break.

### Deployment 2: Understanding the Model

The session is 30 minutes of discussion followed by 30 minutes of presentation.

**Opening.** Briefly outline the story of deployment: by this point, the model mostly is what it is. The focus shifts from shaping the model to understanding and constraining the whole deployed system — the model, but also everything around it. You're now iterating: test, deploy, observe, improve.

**Discussion (30 min).** The goal is to get students to reinvent the main deployment-time methods. When a student proposes something that matches a real method, name it, write it on a sticky note, and put it on the board.

Open with: "You've finished training your model. You suspect it might have problems you haven't anticipated — maybe hidden tendencies, maybe behaviors that only appear in specific contexts. What do you do?"

*Block 1: Monitoring intent.* Before diving into specific methods, pose the question: "We said we care not just about the model doing the right thing, but doing it for the right reasons. How would you check that?" Let students brainstorm — they may suggest looking at the chain of thought, asking the model to explain itself, or inspecting internal representations. If someone proposes CoT monitoring, push on its limitations: "What if the model lies in its chain of thought? What if it learns to hide its reasoning because you're monitoring it?" Let the tension develop — CoT is the best signal we have, but it's fragile. If a student suggests looking at activations directly, validate it and note that we'll return to this later. Close the block by naming the core tradeoff: CoT monitoring works now, but there's a risk that optimizing against it teaches the model to obfuscate, so we may need to accept a "monitorability tax" — deliberately not training against CoT content to keep it honest.

*Block 2: Auditing.* Let students propose ways to test the model. They'll likely suggest running benchmarks. Validate this, then push further: "Benchmarks only test what you think to test for. How do you find problems you didn't anticipate?" Steer toward the idea of generating diverse, realistic scenarios and observing the model's behavior — automated auditing. Ask: "Could you automate the scenario generation? What about the analysis — could a model audit another model?" Name Petri and Bloom. Then ask: "How do you know your auditing methods are good enough? How would you test them?" Steer toward the red-teaming game idea — training a model with a known hidden objective and seeing if your methods catch it.

*Block 3: Post-deployment observation.* Ask: "The model is now deployed and millions of people are using it. You want to understand what they're doing with it — both for safety and to understand the model's impact on society. But you can't read their conversations. How do you analyze usage patterns without violating privacy?" Let students discover the idea of using the model itself to analyze and cluster conversations — automated, privacy-preserving summarization. Name Clio.

*Block 4: Runtime defenses.* Ask: "Your audit found a problem — the model sometimes helps users with dangerous requests that slip past its training. You could go back and retrain, but that takes weeks and massive compute. Is there something faster and more modular you could do?" Steer toward the idea of adding external classifiers around the model that filter inputs and outputs. Ask: "What would you train them on? How would you keep them cheap to run?" Name constitutional classifiers.

**Presentation (30 min).** Walk through the content section, following the slides. Repeat the overarching story: deployment is an ongoing loop — test, deploy, observe, improve. The model is mostly fixed, so we gain leverage by increasing our visibility into it and adding modular defenses around it. Whenever you reach a method that students proposed during the discussion, point to their sticky note and make the connection explicit. Present the methods in order: CoT monitoring as the cross-cutting primitive; pre-deployment auditing to find unknown problems; post-deployment conversation analysis to understand real-world behavior; runtime classifiers and trajectory monitors as modular, updatable defenses. Close by repeating the high-level story: deployment-time alignment is not a one-shot test but a cycle of observation and improvement, and the key advantage of external safety systems is that they can be updated cheaply without retraining the model.

### Deployment 3: Building the Safety System

The session is 30 minutes of discussion followed by 30 minutes of presentation.

**Opening.** Briefly outline the story of red-teaming. We now have a trained model and an understanding of what it can do and where it might be fragile, and we want to deploy it.

**Discussion (30 min).** The goal is to get students to reinvent the structure of the deployed safety system and the basic taxonomy of red-teaming methods. When a student proposes something that matches a real method, name it, write it on a sticky note, and put it on the board.

*Block 1: Runtime safeguards.* Set up the scenario: you've deployed a model and discovered that it sometimes complies with a kind of harmful request you trained it to refuse. What do you do? Someone will likely propose going back and retraining. Push on this: retraining is slow, expensive, and hard to even reproduce reliably for some failures, and you've already partially deployed the model. Steer toward the idea of adding something external to the model that runs at inference time and can be patched cheaply when problems show up. Once they're there, ask what shape this external thing should take — let them propose input/output classifiers, and name Constitutional Classifiers when they do. Then ask: these run on every query, so what becomes important? Steer toward inference cost, and mention the two solutions (hierarchies of cheap-to-expensive classifiers, and reusing the base model's own activations) without dwelling on either. Finally, ask: does scoring single outputs make sense for an agent that takes many steps with tools? Let them discover trajectory monitoring, and name OpenAI's coding agent monitors. If time allows, mention access controls as a forward-looking idea that doesn't apply one global policy to every query.

*Block 2: How do we even talk about safety?* Pose the question directly: suppose you've built this whole safety system. How would you tell someone — yourself, a regulator, the public — whether it's actually safe? Let students struggle for a minute. They will likely propose benchmarks or evaluation suites; accept this, then push: what does it mean to "pass"? Steer toward the realization that we cannot prove anything, and that what we can do is report *how much adversarial effort the system withstood before breaking*. Name this as the central idea of the module: safety claims are effort-bounded statistical claims, not proofs. Mention briefly that internal red teams at the labs and external bodies like UK AISI run open-ended attempts and report how long it took experts to find a universal jailbreak.

*Block 3: How do we put pressure on the system?* Ask: imagine you're the red team, how would you actually try to break the model? Let students brainstorm. Steer the conversation by access level. Start with: what if the model's weights are public — what can you do then? Don't try to make them invent the refusal direction paper, but lead them to the realization that with full access, the attacker can directly find and disable whatever internal mechanism causes refusal, and that this is essentially a solved problem. Note the consequence: open-weight model safety can't really be refusal training, it has to be unlearning, and unlearning isn't robust. Then move to black box: only queries, no weights. Let students propose narrative attacks (grandma exploit) and name them, then push: are there attacks that don't try to *persuade* the model at all, but just exploit the fact that it's a piece of statistical software? Hint if needed: what happens if you push the model out of the distribution it was trained on? Steer toward very long contexts (many-shot jailbreaking), simple resampling (best-of-N), and padded reasoning (CoT hijacking), naming each as it comes up. Close the block with transfer: ask whether anyone has heard of the platonic representation hypothesis, and once it's named, ask what an attacker could do with the fact that different models trained similarly have similar internal representations. Steer them to the answer: optimize an attack against an open-source model where you have white-box access, and deploy it against the closed-source target.

**Presentation (30 min).** Walk through the content section, following the slides. Repeat the overarching story: we cannot prove the system is safe, so we build defenses and then put adversarial pressure on them, and the strength of every safety claim is exactly the strength of the red-teaming behind it. Whenever you reach a method that students proposed during the discussion, point to their sticky note on the board and make the connection explicit. The narrative spine is: runtime safeguards → the effort-bounded notion of safety → access-level taxonomy of jailbreaks → the takeaway. Time is tight, so prioritize this spine over any individual paper — if you have to cut, cut details about specific methods rather than the conceptual transitions between sections. If you have time, cover the fine-tuning API access level briefly here even though it didn't come up in the discussion (Jailbreak-Tuning is the key pointer). Close by repeating the high-level story one more time: a deployed model never stands on its own, but is surrounded by a safety *system*. The system is never proven safe, only empirically resistant to a measured amount of effort, and that number is what every safety claim ultimately rests on.
learningOutcomes: Name the main stages of LLM development — pre-training, post-training, and deployment — and understand what allowances each of the stages gives us in regards to model safety.
Name the main alignment methods used in each phase, and intuitively understand the throughlines that connect the methods within each phase.
Feel at home reading most applied alignment papers.

frontmatter.summary: The theoretical foundations of reward learning — how RLHF can (in principle) recover an aligned objective, when underspecification and misspecification break that story, and how assistance games reframe the problem.

frontmatter.title: Principles of Learning
frontmatter.summary: A broad overview of the three fundamental barriers to universal learning — approximation, generalization, and optimization — and how Solomonoff induction frames them.
frontmatter.internalNotes:
### Some notes on intentions here

- There's a tradeoff between breadth and depth in what we cover: these first two learning theory days are something like a broad but shallow overview, whereas the next three days are more in-depth "case studies" on specific research directions
- Trying to avoid the failure mode where we just focus on particular hobby horses of existing researchers in the safety community - we want them to be aware of at least the basic phenomena the field is trying to explain
- Explicitly, the goal here is "awareness," not deep knowledge, unlike the "case study" days later in the sequence. What we cover will connect directly to later days, which explore particular solutions in depth
learningOutcomes: Students can explain the three fundamental barriers to building a universal learning machine: approximation, generalization, and optimization, and articulate why they are sometimes/naively in tension with each other

Students can explain what Solomonoff induction is, how it resolves the approximation and generalization barriers via a universal hypothesis class and simplicity prior, and why it fails on optimization (note that students are not expected to know the low-level details of Solomonoff induction, just understand the basic setup; students are aware of the optimality result for Solomonoff induction)
Students can explain the bias-variance tradeoff and why it might suggest universal learning is impossible, and how the simplicity prior in Solomonoff induction sidesteps this
Students can state the no free lunch theorem and explain what it does and does not imply about practical learning
Students can explain, at a high level, why even polynomial-time hypothesis classes can be hard to learn over efficiently (the PRG/cryptographic hardness argument)
Students can articulate why learning requires structure in the world, and what this implies about what we should expect from powerful learning machines

Students understand the relevance of this material to AGI safety: if AGI is a learning machine, the three barriers constrain what it can look like, and Solomonoff induction serves as a theoretical reference point for reasoning about its capabilities
